issues.title: TextFormField focus consumes enormous system resources on simulator & iPhone
comments:
Thanks for the detailed report @SexyBeast007 
I see there's another related / potentially similar issue with focused textfield when `readOnly: true`. https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/108187. Can I suggest you to please check if using `readOnly: true` in `TextFormField` too gives you same behavior as you originally reported ? That way, we can possibly club this issue with the linked one for easier tracking.

@darshankawar - just ran the Flutter textFormField demo app with textFormField property readOnly: true

Same exact behaviour as without readOnly. Spikes CPU resources and in a short while a powerful macbook pro becomes almost unusable. It must be creating some sort of loop unseen at a high level.

So yes same issue as what you've referenced. I'm amazed at how nobody else has encountered this issue or at least reported it. I do suspect that in release mode the issue is less prevalent or as noticeable. But I have yet to test release mode with dev tools or xcode cpu profiling.

Please tell me there is some hope for this issue to be resolved. All I can do is wait and hope, I'm definitely not competent enough to assist in this especially at lower levels of code. At least not competent enough yet, or else I would.

On iOS, the native behavior is to animate the opacity of the cursor and not just toggle it on and off. Flutter implements this behavior, which means that while a text field is focused, an animation has to be constantly running. https://github.com/flutter/flutter/pull/104335 probably made the animation more efficient.

It's not surprising that this requires a lot of resources, more so on older devices.

Thanks for the feedback. It looks related / similar to https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/108187 and https://github.com/flutter/flutter/pull/106893 could be a factor.

Just a heads-up that a workaround was added to a recent release of Flutter exposing `cursorOpacityAnimates:` on `TextField` (but not yet `TextFormField`).  Setting that arg to `false` will eliminate the crazy CPU usage with hardly any discernable visual difference between cursors.

https://stackoverflow.com/a/76526057/2301224

It sounds like there are two things going on in this issue:
* The blinking cursor is expensive. That's #59327.

  This is also what the workaround @ben-xx mentions above is for.

* The original report, and at least one [subsequent report above](https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/113192#issuecomment-1369342032), are in debug mode.

  In general, Flutter's performance is very different between debug mode and release mode. As a result, it's rarely a good idea to measure a Flutter app's performance in debug mode, because the answers will be very different from those in release mode, and you should always be shipping release builds to your users. For docs, see: https://docs.flutter.dev/testing/build-modes

  Instead, to measure performance you should use release mode or profile mode. Profile mode is close to release mode, with just enough debugging to enable performance profiling.

I also encountered the same problem,
TextField takes up a lot of CPU resources when it gets focus (pops up the keyboard) and when it gives up focus (closes the keyboard), resulting in an obvious lag of about 2 seconds.

After testing, I found that the problem should be the keyboard. I tested it on the simulator and the real machine respectively, and found that as long as the keyboard is popped up and closed, it will occupy a lot of CPU resources. On the simulator, the TextField does not pop up the keyboard after acquiring the input focus. It is relatively normal. If the keyboard pops up, it will occupy a lot of CPU resources. When the keyboard is closed, it will occupy more and the lag will be more obvious. Therefore, it is guessed that a large amount of CPU resources are consumed when rebuilding the UI.

issues.title: RawKeyboard listener not working as expected when holding a key on Android Emulator
issues.body:
RawKeyboard listener don't seem to produce the correct behavior when a key is hold down, the callback keeps getting called with key down and key up events, even when the key is not released yet. I have tested on beta and master channel. 

## Steps to Reproduce

<!-- You must include full steps to reproduce so that we can reproduce the problem. -->

1. Run `flutter create bug`.
2. Update the main method to the following code:

```dart
void main() {
  WidgetsFlutterBinding.ensureInitialized();
  RawKeyboard.instance.addListener((e) {
    print(e);
  });
  runApp(MyApp());
}
```

**Expected results:**
When I hold a key down, it should print a key down event and when I release the key, a key up should be printed

**Actual results:**
Multiple key up and key downs are printed one after another as long as the key is pressed, like this log below:

cc @gspencergoog

I am trying to look on this issue, and I discovered where this behavior originates, I discovered that this simultaneously triggering of the events happens on the `dispatchKeyEvent` of the [FlutterView class](https://github.com/flutter/engine/blob/78b567f7d55b66a6e9bcd59a8703202c1b8a6557/shell/platform/android/io/flutter/embedding/android/FlutterView.java#L735).

I have made a small native android example to test, and it seems that this behavior that we are seeing, is indeed how the android behaves on that method, this is the code I made to test:

```kotlin
package com.example.mykeyboardexample

import android.os.Bundle
import android.util.Log
import android.view.KeyEvent
import androidx.appcompat.app.AppCompatActivity

class MainActivity : AppCompatActivity() {

    override fun onCreate(savedInstanceState: Bundle?) {
        super.onCreate(savedInstanceState)
    }

    override fun dispatchKeyEvent(event: KeyEvent?): Boolean {
        val type = if (event?.action == KeyEvent.ACTION_DOWN) "Down" else "Up";
        Log.d("AAAA",  " ${event?.keyCode} ${type} ${event?.repeatCount.toString()}");
        return super.dispatchKeyEvent(event)
    }
}
```

And the output on logcat is pretty much what we are seeing on Flutter

```
2020-12-23 16:40:02.939 28917-28917/com.example.mykeyboardexample D/AAAA:  29 Down 0
2020-12-23 16:40:02.942 28917-28917/com.example.mykeyboardexample D/AAAA:  29 Up 0
2020-12-23 16:40:06.231 28917-28917/com.example.mykeyboardexample D/AAAA:  29 Down 0
2020-12-23 16:40:06.232 28917-28917/com.example.mykeyboardexample D/AAAA:  29 Up 0
2020-12-23 16:40:06.731 28917-28917/com.example.mykeyboardexample D/AAAA:  29 Down 0
2020-12-23 16:40:06.732 28917-28917/com.example.mykeyboardexample D/AAAA:  29 Up 0
2020-12-23 16:40:06.815 28917-28917/com.example.mykeyboardexample D/AAAA:  29 Down 0
2020-12-23 16:40:06.816 28917-28917/com.example.mykeyboardexample D/AAAA:  29 Up 0
2020-12-23 16:40:06.899 28917-28917/com.example.mykeyboardexample D/AAAA:  29 Down 0
...
```

I am going to continue investigating this and will report if I find anything new.

I did further investigation, and I discovered that when testing on a real device, this runs as expected  (my first tests were executed on an emulator), I have connected a bluetooth keyboard on a real device and now when I press down and hold a key, a get various key down events with the correct repeat count, but only once I release the key I get a key up event. This is the log of my test on a real device with a bluetooth keyboard:

```
I/flutter (19754): Up - Repeat: 0
I/flutter (19754): Down - Repeat: 0
I/flutter (19754): Down - Repeat: 1
I/flutter (19754): Down - Repeat: 2
I/flutter (19754): Down - Repeat: 3
I/flutter (19754): Down - Repeat: 4
...
I/flutter (19754): Up - Repeat: 0
```
For that test I have updated my step to reproduce code to only print if it is a key down or up and the repeat count.

So I am confident that this is not an issue with flutter, but an issue with Android Emulators. Before closing this, I just wonder if we should add any hint about this weird behavior of android emulators somewhere in the docs?
Emulators are already pretty well known to have many differences from actual hardware, especially where input is concerned. It wouldn't hurt to add a comment in the docs explaining that, though.

I also can reproduce this issue on the latest `stable` and `master` channels with Android Emulator. As above investigation and comments, I'm going to update appropriate labels for this issue.

```dart
import 'package:flutter/material.dart';
```

```
import 'package:flutter/material.dart';

void main() {
  runApp(MaterialApp(
    home: SearchInputWidget(onSubmitted: (String text) { print(text); }, preInputText: 'Test',)
  ));
}

class SearchInputWidget extends StatefulWidget {
  final String preInputText;
  final Function(String text) onSubmitted;

  const SearchInputWidget({
    required this.onSubmitted,
    required this.preInputText
  });

  @override
  State<StatefulWidget> createState() {
    return new _SearchInputWidgetState();
  }
}

class _SearchInputWidgetState extends State<SearchInputWidget> {
  bool showCancel = false;
  String inputText = "";

  @override
  void initState() {
    super.initState();
    this.inputText = (widget.preInputText != null ? widget.preInputText : "");

  }

  @override
  Widget build(BuildContext context) {
    print("searchInput build "+ this.inputText);
    TextEditingController inputController = TextEditingController.fromValue(TextEditingValue(
        text: inputText,
        selection: TextSelection.fromPosition(TextPosition(
            affinity: TextAffinity.downstream,
            offset: '${inputText}'.length)
        )
    ));
    return Scaffold(
      body: Center(
        child: Container(
          //margin: EdgeInsets.only(left: 40, top: 40),
          alignment: Alignment(0, 0),
          height: 44,
          width: 300,
          decoration: new BoxDecoration(
            color: Colors.grey.shade200,
            borderRadius: BorderRadius.all(Radius.circular(12.0)),
            //border: new Border.all(width: 1, color: Colors.red),
          ),
          child: new TextField(
            //maxLines: 1,
            controller: inputController,
            maxLength: 30,
            cursorColor: Colors.black,
            style: TextStyle(fontSize: 16, color: Colors.black),
            onChanged: (text){
              print("searchInput onChanged "+ text);
              setState(() {
                print("searchInput setState "+ text);
                inputText = text;
                showCancel = (text.length > 0);
              });
            },
            onSubmitted: (text) {
              widget.onSubmitted != null && widget.onSubmitted(text);
            },
            decoration: InputDecoration(
              prefixIcon: Icon(
                Icons.search,
                color: Colors.black45,
                size: 24,
              ),
              suffixIcon: new Offstage(
                offstage: !showCancel,
                child: new IconButton(
                    icon: Icon(
                      Icons.cancel,
                      color: Colors.black45,
                      size: 20,
                    ),
                    onPressed: _onClickClear),
              ),
              border: InputBorder.none,
              /*  fillColor: Colors.red,
                    filled: true,*/
              counterText: "",
            ),
          ),
        ),
      )
    );
  }
  void _onClickClear() {
    setState(() {
      inputText = "";
      showCancel = false;
    });
  }
}

```

</details>

issues.title: [iOS][Snapshotting] Snapshotting a view that has not been rendered at least once requires afterScreenUpdates:YES.
issues.body:
<!-- Thank you for using Flutter!

     If you are looking for support, please check out our documentation
     or consider asking a question on Stack Overflow:

     to what you're looking for, then fill out the template below. Please read
     our guide to filing a bug first: https://flutter.dev/docs/resources/bug-reports
-->
When I tried to make the text input of a text field start with a capital letter by default and tested it on a physical iOS device, I got the following message in the debug console, after calling `TextEditingController().clear()`:
```
[Snapshotting] Snapshotting a view (0x104e3dd80, _UIReplicantView) that has not been rendered at least once requires afterScreenUpdates:YES.
```


## Steps to Reproduce

<!-- You must include full steps to reproduce so that we can reproduce the problem. -->

1. Run `flutter create bug`.
2. Update the `main.dart` as follows: .
```dart
import 'package:flutter/material.dart';

void main() {
  runApp(MyApp());
}

class MyApp extends StatelessWidget {
  @override
  Widget build(BuildContext context) {
    return MaterialApp(
      theme: ThemeData(
        primarySwatch: Colors.blue,
      ),
      home: MyHomePage(),
    );
  }
}

class MyHomePage extends StatelessWidget {
  final controller = TextEditingController();
  @override
  Widget build(BuildContext context) {
    return Scaffold(
      body: Center(
        child: Column(
          mainAxisAlignment: MainAxisAlignment.center,
          children: <Widget>[
            TextField(
              controller: controller,
              textCapitalization: TextCapitalization.sentences,
            ),
            TextButton(
              onPressed: controller.clear,
              child: Text('Clear TextField'),
            ),
          ],
        ),
      ),
    );
  }
}

```
3. Run the App on a physical iOS device
Type anything into the text field and press `Clear TextField` which calls `TextEditingController().clear()`

**Expected results:** <!-- what did you want to see? -->
A cleared TextField.
**Actual results:** <!-- what did you see? -->
A cleared Textfield and a message in the debug-console saying:
```
[Snapshotting] Snapshotting a view (0x104e3dd80, _UIReplicantView) that has not been rendered at least once requires afterScreenUpdates:YES.
```

issues.title: TextField cursor handle rendered below keyboard and disappearing after dragging it
issues.body:
## Problem Description
It is best to see the problem in action:
[CursorBug.mp4.zip](https://github.com/flutter/flutter/files/5361621/CursorBug.mp4.zip)

As you can see, the cursor handle is not above the system keyboard.
And when dragging it, it just reset itself and stop responding to the dragging. so you need to tap again to drag it again.

```

  textInputAction: TextInputAction.send, 
  keyboardType: TextInputType.text,
```

comments:
Hi @naamapps 
Can you please provide your `flutter doctor -v`, a minimal complete reproducible code sample that I can just copy/paste and run it?
Thank you

@pedromassangocode I think that the cursor handle is not really native. In other apps when dragging the handle, my phone vibrates (haptic feedback). In flutter it's like a widget made in flutter that replicates the behavior of the native cursor.
That is why it's also rendering below the keyboard and I have no haptic feedback. 
Maybe it's better to make it native somehow.
Thanks

This is currently the expected behavior since we draw those handles ourselves and we cannot draw outside of our own window. Since the keyboard is not part of our window, we cannot draw over it.

@gspencergoog Not sure if the multi-window stuff could potentially fix this on Android?
I doubt it. The keyboard is a separate app that the system layers on top of ours, and I don't think we're allowed to draw over it.

>However, even if it's not possible to turn it into a native text field - there is still a bug where the handles disappear when >dragging them.

@goderbauer should we close this and open a new issue about the other unexpected behavior?

issues.title: Add desktop shell support for text navigation key combinations
issues.body:
On macOS, typing cmd+right invokes moveToRightEndOfLine: These methods are already received by doCommandBySelector: so we we just need to implement the logic.

On glfw we could use modifier flags https://www.glfw.org/docs/latest/group__mods.html#ga14994d3196c290aaa347248e51740274
comments: To clarify, the original description here is one such shortcut, but there are many that we should support across the desktop platforms. Other examples include things like forward/back word, delete word, etc.
See #34949 for some more specific cases that should be supported.
Paste shortcuts do not work either.
I'm curious: why do these key bindings need to be added on the engine side?  Why can't we handle these keys on the framework side and just have it tell the shell how to modify the selection, etc.?

Well, on macOS at least there's
https://developer.apple.com/library/archive/documentation/Cocoa/Conceptual/EventOverview/TextDefaultsBindings/TextDefaultsBindings.html
("To customize bindings, you create a file named DefaultKeyBinding.dict in ~/Library/KeyBindings/ and specify bindings to augment or replace the standard bindings.")

So we either need to create logic in the engine that understands all the customization options that each OS understands and then feed those to the framework at launch (and ideally watch for changes), or we need to let the OS handle that abstraction layer for us. The latter should be easier and more robust.

(Or we decide that we will not support any OS-level customization of keyboard handling, but that will permanently put us at a disadvantage relative to a standard Cocoa application.)

But don't we still have the problem then that we need to know what all of the bound operations and their key combinations for each OS are and support those on the framework side then?

Hypothetically, in this scenario, I should be thinking of building a `MoveWordForwardAndModifySelectionAction` in the framework that can then be invoked by a system channel message from the platform, right?

What happens if an app wants to change the binding of "command-rightArrow"  on macOS and "control-rightArrow" on Linux to execute an action of their choice?  Do they have to instead override and replace the `MoveWordForwardAndModifySelectionAction`, and ignore the "command-rightArrow" key event on macOS, but only if it matches the key binding on the platform side, and bind the key themselves if it isn't bound on the platform side?

Is there a macOS API to get the keys mapped in the bindings? Maybe we can make it more automatic by checking the list of system bindings, and doing the right thing in the shortcut manager?

I'm not sure I follow. Cursor and selection mutation are currently handled in the engine layer, so I'm not sure why this would need custom actions in the framework. This bug is just about the fact that we currently don't actually perform the relevant mutations for many NSResponder methods.

We do the cursor and selection mutation in the engine?  What about this code:
https://github.com/flutter/flutter/blob/master/packages/flutter/lib/src/rendering/editable.dart#L441

Isn't that doing the cursor and selection mutation? I mean, I just rewrote that code, but I was just converting it to use `LogicalKeyboardKey` instead of Android key events, I didn't really change the functionality.

I'm not sure where that fits in. https://github.com/flutter/engine/blob/master/shell/platform/darwin/macos/framework/Source/FlutterTextInputPlugin.mm was necessary for text input to work (similar code exists in the other desktop platforms) and it's managing cursor and selection.

OK, I spoke with Jason, and he told me how these things relate.  I think the quote was something like "basically, they both try and mutate the text, and then fling updates at each other to stay in sync."  It seems like the two systems are supposed to cooperatively and asynchronously edit the same text, which makes it hard to decide who gets to set the key bindings, since the engine might have some set of bindings that mutate the text (that it maybe absorbs and doesn't send to the framework), and the framework could be looking for a different binding that might also mutate the text, but doesn't match the platform bindings.
And, for instance, who gets to do the "cut/copy/paste" mutations?  I guess I'll take a look at the Android code in the engine and see what it does in those cases.
We want content modifications to be visible enough to the Dart layer to support undo.
modifier + backspace was brought up in https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/74316 as another combination we should support.

I have a question is why we have to process text in the shell and not in the flutter. what if process large text strings? it not optimized about memory because shell away call "setText" function to puts string and send it to flutter
I think answer is mostly for legacy reasons right now.  There are a lot of parts of text editing that I'd like to move into the Flutter framework if I get time.

> it not optimized about memory because shell away call "setText" function to puts string and send it to flutter

Strings will always need to exist in both the shell and the framework in order to interact with IMEs and accessibility.

Moving more processing to the framework is a goal, but not because of memory.

@justinmc Yeah, it still needs engine work, and once that's done, we may need to revise the defaults on the framework side too: we should probably be sending a message to the framework to invoke an action for these editing events instead of sending a key event for some of these methods, and removing the defaults for macOS on the framework.  That would allow a Mac with customized key bindings or a different key layout to do the right thing.

cc @cbracken

It would also hopefully allow keyboard navigation on the accent selection popup window, which doesn't work currently. For this to work the keyboard navigation must be handled through `_textInputContext handleEvent` and then passed to framework from `NSStandardKeyBindingResponding` methods.
@justinmc @LongCatIsLooong can you update on the current state of implementation in the framework? Could one of you add an itemised list of remaining tasks that need to be completed?

I think [this comment](https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/30725#issuecomment-797830058) above is the best summary of the work to be done here (CC @gspencergoog).  If I understand correctly, some work needs to be done for the engine to receive these shortcuts, and then the [Mac shortcuts in the framework](https://github.com/flutter/flutter/blob/bac1af32eebec43b43bb9046c0046524b7a22c59/packages/flutter/lib/src/widgets/default_text_editing_shortcuts.dart#L375) need to be readjusted.

For the record all of these text navigation shortcuts should already work, as they are now handled in the framework.  But this issue is about handling them in the engine to be able to work with things like customized key bindings.

Here's an itemized list, but correct me if I'm wrong:

 * [ ] Mac engine receives text navigation keyboard shortcuts and communicates them to the framework.
 * [ ] Mac framework text navigation keyboard shortcut handling removed ([here](https://github.com/flutter/flutter/blob/bac1af32eebec43b43bb9046c0046524b7a22c59/packages/flutter/lib/src/widgets/default_text_editing_shortcuts.dart#L375)).
@justinmc @LongCatIsLooong we're punting this as a known issue to the post-stable project.

#78061 likely depends on this. 

To allow IME menus (and accent menus etc.) to properly function, every relevant key event must be sent to `-[NSTextInputContext handleEvent:]` (or `-[NSResponder interepretKeyEvents:]`) before being sent to the framework. But unfortunately AppKit seems to bundle dispatching key events to the IME and shortcut management in that same public method, and to make matters worse `-[NSTextInputContext handleEvent:]` seems to almost always return true (even when there's no defined shortcut, `doCommandBySelector:` isn't getting called, and there's no candidate menu). So if we send all key events to `NSTextInputContext` we won't be able to define shortcuts for input fields using the `Shortcuts` widget, as the key events are almost always consumed by the system and the framework won't get a chance to handle them.

So far as I understand it the options are:
#### 1.Let macOS `NSKeybindingManager` does the key sequence -> user intent mapping. The Flutter framework will receive messages that are roughly equivalent to text editing `Intent`s from the engine instead of key messages. 

This should be the easiest of the 3 options to implement and maintain, and will probably result in the best end user experience as the text editing keybindings will be consistent across the system. But for developers that want to create cross platform apps and are not familiar with how keybindings work on macOS this will be a bit confusing. And if someone wants to create a sophisticated text editor with custom/customizable keybindings they won't be able to.

#### 2.Keep collecting key events in the text input plugin and when `doCommandBySelector:` is called, figure out the key sequences that triggered the selector, and send both the key sequence and the selector name to the framework

This way the framework gets to decide whether to perform the selectors suggested by the macOS input system, or map the key sequence to a different framework Intent, which is optimal. But this relies on the assumption that `doCommandBySelector:` is always called synchronously by `-[NSTextInputContext handleEvent:] `. Also figuring out the key sequence can be difficult as you can define emacs-esque keybindings on macOS (e.g. for <kbd>C-x</kbd> <kbd>k</kbd>, the <kbd>C-x</kbd> prefix is probably not going to trigger a `doCommandBySelector:` call and it will be difficult to figure out it's a part of the shortcut that's associated with the current selector when the final trigger key <kbd>k</kbd> is pressed).

#### 3.Preprocess the key events to decide whether it should be sent to the framework or `NSTextInputContext`

This is what [emacs](https://github.com/emacs-mirror/emacs/blob/5f5189e9be6c70c4db99e8057287d16955b9c620/src/nsterm.m#L6227-L6255) and [iTerm2](https://github.com/gnachman/iTerm2/blob/master/sources/iTermKeyboardHandler.m#L58-L115) do. As far as my test goes IMEs that require text composition (from macOS, not the emacs builtin ones) work. But when I press and hold a vow in most native macOS text editing application it brings up the accent selection menu but in emacs it doesn't. This approach is probably going to be slightly harder to maintain than approach 1 because of features like the accent menu. But for developer that are building a sophisticated text editor this is probably a requirement to be able to have app-specific text editing shortcuts. 

I think for now it should be fairly straight forward to adopt the first approach and add documentation to the `Shortcuts` widget that says for macOS text editing currently only the system-wide shortcuts are available (and that will fix #78061). In the future we can add a flag to allow developer to opt-in 3 if they want fully customized shortcuts. 2 is ideal but unfortunately I still can't find a way to implement that and it seems difficult to implement and maintain. WDYT?

This is great analysis, thanks for digging up all of this.  I think solving this is probably the right way to solve all of these Mac composing issues: https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/78061, https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/91859, https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/85328

Your comment was written in terms of Mac but will this be the approach for all other platforms too?  So does solution no. 1 include moving all of the framework keyboard shortcut handling with Shortcuts and moving it to these new Intent-ish messages for all platforms?

Also does your solution no. 1 always call `[NSTextInputContext handleEvent:]`?  Does it not call `flutter/keyevent` if it detects a keyboard shortcut with `NSKeybindingManager`?

I agree that 1 is not ideal but it should be straightforward to transition from 1 to 2 or 3 if we decide to do that.

> will this be the approach for all other platforms too

I think macOS is unique regarding text editing shortcuts handling in the sense that the `-[NSTextInputContext handleEvent:]` method always (as far as I can tell) claim the incoming event for itself, should we decide to route a key event to that method. This wouldn't have been a problem if the said method correctly returns false if it does not handle a key event. 

> Also does your solution no. 1 always call [NSTextInputContext handleEvent:]?

Yes and if a text field is the current responder no key events will be sent to the framework. This is actually kinda similar to how soft keyboards work on other platforms. I was thinking maybe mac catalyst does not have the this problem.

Got it.  So we wouldn't have to remove the whole Shortcuts setup for text editing keyboard shortcuts, but it would be a strange experience that it would stop working for Mac while continuing to work for all other platforms.

It looks like this same problem does exist on iOS as well in the case that you connect a physical keyboard to a physical device.  I was suspicious that it would because the framework handles things like the delete key similarly on iOS ([code](https://github.com/flutter/flutter/blob/2d8b8e12aa7eebb4b429e5c7d60f8414c38e0533/packages/flutter/lib/src/widgets/default_text_editing_shortcuts.dart#L270)) and the order of primary and secondary key responders seems the same in the iOS engine ([code](https://github.com/flutter/engine/blob/684cc4778115f97fce0fb86282c7832dc1c68b7f/shell/platform/darwin/ios/framework/Source/FlutterViewController.mm#L669-L673)), and indeed after testing it I get the same behavior that the IME isn't updated by physical keyboard keys.  This doesn't happen with the simulator and a physical keyboard, I'm guessing because it's treated as a soft keyboard.

It's probably not as high priority as fixing this for Mac, but I imagine there are many people using physical keyboards on iPad.
@gspencergoog @stuartmorgan I'm curious what you guys think about the approaches in https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/30725#issuecomment-948063626.

~~When https://github.com/flutter/flutter/pull/80899 is merged, web will also inherit these problems (at least on Mac/iOS).~~

*Update:* That PR was closed, but still something to keep in mind if that's the plan for web long term.

Webkit seems to simply call private APIs for sending key events to the IME: https://github.com/WebKit/WebKit/blob/56c4388c36c4752b25d959dc881d89183f93aee4/Source/WebKit/UIProcess/Cocoa/WebViewImpl.mm#L5072-L5087

Chromium does something more complex which is similar to the approach 2 described in the previous comment, but it requires some intricate bookkeeping and edge case handling: https://source.chromium.org/chromium/chromium/src/+/main:content/app_shim_remote_cocoa/render_widget_host_view_cocoa.mm;l=904-1135?q=_handlingKeyDown&ss=chromium%2Fchromium%2Fsrc (it seems `doCommandBySelector:` is invoked synchronously by `interpretKeyEvents:`. Since the application has to communicate with the IME via IPC I thought it had to be asynchronous?).
Also they had to implement [a method that seems to be private](https://source.chromium.org/chromium/chromium/src/+/main:content/app_shim_remote_cocoa/render_widget_host_view_cocoa.mm;l=880-890?q=doCommandBySelector:&ss=chromium%2Fchromium%2Fsrc) to override key interface control (https://developer.apple.com/library/archive/documentation/Cocoa/Conceptual/EventOverview/EventArchitecture/EventArchitecture.html#//apple_ref/doc/uid/10000060i-CH3-SW10) so keybindings like <kbd>C-tab</kbd> and <kbd>C-←</kbd> can be properly handled by the browser.

I will try to verify that shortcuts like <kbd>C-x</kbd><kbd>k</kbd> work with this approach since overall it seems better.

In https://github.com/flutter/flutter/blob/bac1af32eebec43b43bb9046c0046524b7a22c59/packages/flutter/lib/src/widgets/default_text_editing_shortcuts.dart#L375

I noticed these (Emacs-derived?) shortcuts are not added for MacOS:
* `ctrl-a` (to start of line)
* `ctrl-e` (to end of line)
* `ctrl-f` (forward one character)
* `ctrl-b` (backward one character)

Any specific reasons for this? 

Not sure if others use these, but for me, it feels kinda jarring when they are not there.
@byw `_macShortcuts` will soon be removed and the macOS system shortcuts will be honored that will hopefully enable emacs shortcuts for text editing on macOS.

To be clear, the current state of this issue is that the default {cmd,option}[-shift]-arrow keyboard shortcuts do work in Flutter text fields, however, the support for personalised keyboard bindings (and the ctrl-a/ctrl-e etc. emacs-like bindings) are not yet wired up.

Bumping this to the post-stable project for tracking there.

issues.title: TextEditingController doesn't keep selection on text changes
issues.body:
**Steps to reproduce:**

- Create TextField with "start value" text with `final tf = TextField(controller: TextEditingController(text: 'start value'));`.
- Tap to a middle of the textfield to set the cursor to the middle of the text (like "start| value").
- Programmatically update text in the textfield with `tf.controller.text = 'new value';`

**Expected result:**

- The cursor stays in the middle of the textfield (like "new v|alue").

**Actual result:**

- The cursor moves to the end on the iOS (like "new value|") and to the start on the Android (like "|new value").

**Extra notes:**

- As I can see per [TextEditingController sources](https://github.com/flutter/flutter/blob/989cf18b0daed99ea7c83226bd76fa39693bf94c/packages/flutter/lib/src/widgets/editable_text.dart#L80) it explicitly drops selection with text update.

I'm using the following code to show end-of-line spaces in the input field (by default they get trimmed):
```
static String hardSpace = '\u{00A0}';

  static void processInput(TextEditingController controller) {
    final TextSelection selection = controller.selection;
    final String text = controller.text;
    if (text.endsWith(' ')) {
      // if last character is a space, use a hard space so that it'll actually show up in the field
      controller.text = text.replaceAll(' ', hardSpace);
    } else {
      // otherwise convert all hard spaces back to spaces so that wrapping occurs correctly
      controller.text = text.replaceAll(hardSpace, ' ');
    }
    controller.selection = selection;
  }
```

So, using the technique for keeping selection mentioned above.

However, there is a noticeable flicker doing this - the caret jumps to the start of the line, and then back to the end - whenever the text materially changes. https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/e782d76e-ccd8-4cc6-83c4-510691b9d301

If you press the Tab key while a letter is being composed (i.e., when it has an underline), the focus jumps to the third TextField.

Somehow, your TextField doesn’t enter the composing state after the first attempt, so you might not be able to reproduce the issue.

In my video, you can see what happens when the letter is in the composing state:

- On the first and second attempts, after I finished composing the letter, I pressed the Tab key — the focus jumped directly to the third TextField.
- On the third attempt, I manually ended the composing state by pressing the right arrow key, then pressed Tab — this time, the focus moved to the second TextField.

---

(*) In Korean input, a letter is in the "composing state" when the user is still entering a combination of consonants and vowels — the character is shown with an underline until it's finalized.

---

I guess a debug flag such as "debugPaintLineBoxes" can be helpful for identifying/debugging text vertical alignment issues?

Makes sense.

What do you think about perhaps having an overflow warning, but with a flag so that it can be disabled? And then turn that flag off on the internal apps that see failures. If that'd be doable, then I think it would solve the problem well.

A debug flag that isn't enabled by default (including one for painting debugging information on the screen) would still be helpful — if nothing else, it'd make for an easier recommended action that can be given on this issue and #51257 for developers who run into these symptoms. But to go all the way to resolving this as a stumbling block for developers, I think it'd need to be something on by default. Otherwise it's hard when seeing these symptoms as a developer to even realize that this is the question they should be asking.

I believe https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/139762 ends up being another report of this issue. I'm basing that particularly on https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/139762#issuecomment-1944375717 which says:
> The problem is `height: 3` is too high for the text field height.

which sounds like exactly a version of this issue: the text field was overflowing its bounds, that caused undesired symptoms, and there wasn't a straightforward way for the developer to realize that that was the source of the problem.

I've also just edited the title of this issue to reflect what it's turned out to be about, since https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/35706#issuecomment-512574875 and discussed further in the last few comments above.

This issue was assigned to @LongCatIsLooong but has had no status updates in a long time. To remove any ambiguity about whether the issue is being worked on, the assignee was removed.

I think what happened is that the InputDecorator layout algorithm prioritize content padding over the text content even in a single line text field.
So the top content padding pushes the text down when there isn't enough vertical space, making it look off-centered.

Options I can think of:

1. We could change the layout algorithm such that it prioritize the text over the content padding (for example, vertically center the text within the available space, since RenderEditable does not vertically clip the text in single-line mode it would appear as if the content padding is reduced). This will affect many golden tests.
2. Add a `debugInputDecoratorOverflow` flag so which shows a visual indicator similar to the Row/Column overflow indicator when turned on. 

I'm leaning towards option 1 since the overflow warning is usually not very actionable (dynamically adjusting the vertical padding based on the amount of vertical space available would require LayoutBuilder or a custom RenderBox). I'll introduce an opt-in flag to indicate that in a single-line text field the vertical content padding is maintained only when there is enough vertical space, and see if the default value of the flag can be changed to opt-out.

On seeing an overflow warning, I'd think the way to act on it would be:
* If the padding is important, then increase the height.
* If the short height is important, then reduce the padding.

I agree it'd usually be messy if the developer needed to dynamically adjust the padding. What's the situation where that would be needed, though?

The main thing that comes to mind is that one might do that as a way of controlling the vertical alignment. But I believe that's what `textAlignVertical` is for.

issues.title: Calling TextEditingController.clear() does not call formatEditUpdate of formatter
issues.body:
Calling `TextEditingController.clear()` does not call `formatEditUpdate` of TextInputFormatter's.
This makes it difficult to keep the formatter state in sync with TextEditingController. 

Example case:

Some TextInputFormatter allows you to format the phone number in something like (555) 333-44-55, but it also has a method to get the phone in its original form without formatting like 555334455. There is also a field clear button that calls the method TextEditingController.clear(). When you click on this button, the text field will be erased, but this will not call the method formatEditUpdate, so it is still possible to get the value from the formatter. 

There is a way out to force the programmer to also call some cleanup method on the formatter itself, but it is quite difficult to keep track of this.

[restoreSystemUIOverlays]

issues.title: Focus issues when embedding a Flutter app inside an iframe
issues.body:
### Steps to reproduce

I've been investigating an issue with Cmd+C not always correctly working to copy text from embedded DevTools. I think I've finally tracked down what's happening, and I think it may be a Flutter issue. I can reproduce what seems like the same bug using DartPad without any VS Code/Flutter.

The issue seems to be that when some SelectableText is selected (by clicking and dragging or double-clicking), focus is not changed, so pressing Cmd+C is then handled by whatever else had focus at the time. A single left-click first does move focus and then things work correctly.


### Expected results

Selecting text with a drag or double-click should move focus so that pressing Ctrl+C copies the selection.

### Actual results

Focus does not change, so Ctrl+C is handled by another part of the application (in this case the DartPad editor, but when embedded in VS Code, it's the VS Code editor).

### Code sample

Here's an example testing on DartPad using the following code:

```dart
import 'package:flutter/material.dart';

void main() => runApp(MyApp());

class MyApp extends StatelessWidget {
  @override
  Widget build(BuildContext context) {
    return const MaterialApp(
      title: 'Flutter Demo',
      home: Scaffold(body: SelectableText('This is selectable text')),
    );
  }
}
```

### Screenshots or Video



In this first video, I do the following (this works as expected):

1. Select "SelectableText" in the editor on the left
2. Click once in the Flutter app on the right
3. Double-click to select the word "This" in the Flutter app on the right
4. Press Ctrl+C to copy to clipboard
5. Click in the editor on the left
6. Press Ctrl+V to paste to see what was in the clipboard (it was "This" as expected)

https://github.com/flutter/flutter/assets/1078012/e9dfc551-4d43-4879-81b0-5a1b467dd8b4

In this second video, I do the same as above - but without step 2. I do not left-click in the app to give focus to the Flutter app. Instead, I go straight to double-clicking to select "This". However this time, when I paste, I have actually copied "SelectableText" because focus was really still in the left editor.

https://github.com/flutter/flutter/assets/1078012/4c9760ba-507f-4ab5-b449-4d689103bad3

### Logs

_No response_

### Flutter Doctor output

N/A - reproduced on DartPad with Flutter 3.16.5.

https://github.com/flutter/flutter/blob/f949d8eaedf3084224ecff94a36be2eef543abd0/packages/flutter/lib/src/widgets/default_text_editing_shortcuts.dart#L876-L877

SelectableText is backed by EditableText under the hood, which the web embedder handles with an offscreen `<input>` tag. I wonder what happens if we try to reproduce this bug with SelectionArea instead of SelectableText? SelectionArea does not use EditableText under the hood.
